content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 323-5901 322-6441 322-6444

August 4, 1981

Rosanne Coit, Esq.
Attorney at Law
436 Dorothy Avenue
Ventura, CA 93003

Re: Telephone Request for
Advice

Dear Ms. Coit:

Thank you for your recent telephone request for advice.
Normally we give advice only to individuals who have a duty
under our Act.1/ However, your request presents a unique
factual issue, one which we feel deserves our response.

FACTS

As you related them to me over the telephone, the material
facts are:

(1) There is an opening in the City Attorney's Office
of the City of Ventura.

(2) You are an attorney and wish to apply for this
position.

(3) The position is non-civil service and was recently
created in the budget.

(4) The City Attorney is the sole hiring authority for
the position; the City Council has no participation.

(5) Your spouse is also an attorney and the two of you
have a private practice in the City of Ventura, which is
incorporated.

_______________________________

1/ Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18329(b). All references herein are to the
Government Code unless otherwise specified.

Rosanne Coit, Esq.
August 4, 1981
Page Two


     (6) Your spouse rents his own portion of the office
space from one of the Ventura City Councilmembers. You rent
your office space from someone else. Your spouse's rent
exceeds $500 per month and the councilmember's interest in
the real property exceeds $1,000 (the councilmember is actually
a lessee who, in turn, subleases the space to your spouse).

     (7) At the time the new position in the city attorney's
office was created, you had no intention of applying for the
position. Consequently, the councilmember in question had
no reason to expect that you might eventually be employed in
that position.

     (8) The councilmember in question expects to retire at
the end of the current term, which ends January 1, 1982;
however, this decision is not yet finalized.

     (9) The lease with the councilmember for your spouse's
office space expires March, 1982.

     (10) In June, 1982, the City Council will review your
position as part of its budget review.

     (11) Your income will not change significantly if you
leave your private practice to take the city attorney position.

     You pose several questions based upon the foregoing
facts:

     First, was there any conflict on the part of the council-
member in participating in making the decision to create the
new city attorney position?

     Second, are there any potential conflicts in a decision
to hire you for that position?

     Third, assuming that you are hired, are there any potential
conflicts in any City Council decisions affecting your position?

     Fourth, you raise a tangential question of whether the
lease is the source of income to the councilmember.

ANALYSIS

     The Political Reform Act 2/ requires that "no public
official ... shall make, participate in making or in any way

_______________________________
2/ Sections 81001-91014.

.
-

Rosanne Coit, Esq.
August 4, 1981
Page Three

attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest.-'/ Financial interest is defined in
Section 87103 as follows:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally, on:

* * *

(c) Any source of income --- aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.

Under the facts given, your spouse is a source of income
to the councilmember and will remain so until 12 months have
passed from the end of the lease. Under the facts, you may
also be a source of income because of your joint ownership
of the law firm; however, based upon the analysis which
follows, it is not necessary to determine this issue.

One-half of your income is attributable as community
property to your spouse. Consequently, a significant change
in your income will have a material financial effect on your
spouse.-'/ Therefore, any governmental decision which has a
reasonably foreseeable and significant effect on your income
will have a material financial effect on your spouse.-/

Lastly, the sublease is not the source of income to the
councilmember, it is the person who pays the rent.-'/

_________________________
3/ Section 87100.

4/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(C) or (D).
Note that subdivision (b)(3)(D) is more applicable
under these facts since you state that your spouse pays the
rent for that portion of the office, not the law firm. If
it were the law firm, then you as one-half owner would be
considered a source of income to the councilmember. In the
alternative, the financial effect on the corporation of your
job change would easily exceed the (b)(3)(C) test.

5/ Id., and Section 87103.

6/ See our Advice Letter to Honorable Eugene T. Gualco,
September 21, 1976, copy enclosed.

Rosanne Coit, Esq.
August 4, 1981
Page Four


CONCLUSIONS

     Applying the foregoing analysis to the facts at hand,
we reach the following conclusions:

     Question 1: There was no conflict on the part of the
councilmember in creating the new city attorney position.
Neither you nor the councilmember knew at the time of this
decision that you would apply.

     Question 2: There is an intervening decision-maker in
the person of the City Attorney, who makes the hiring decision.
Therefore we see no potential conflicts in the hiring process,
so long as the councilmember is, in fact, uninvolved.

     Question 3: Should the council take up any issue directly
involving your position — its continued existence, classifi-
cation, etc. — while the councilmember in question is still
on the council, then there would be a conflict of interest
if the councilmember participated.

     This would not be true in the following instances:

     (1) If more than 12 months have expired since your
spouse ceased renting from the councilmember, or otherwise
being a source of income in excess of $250.

     (2) If the council were dealing with city-wide across-
the-board pay increases or decreases, as opposed to your
position or the City Attorney's Office alone. In the case
of such city-wide action, the "public generally"1/ exception
would apply.

     (3) If the effect on your income would be so small as
to not have a material financial effect on your spouse.

     Question 4: The sublease from the councilmember to
your spouse is not the source of income, it is your spouse
(or under an alternative analysis you and your spouse).

     We trust that this letter satisfactorily answers your
questions. Should you need clarification please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901. In the event you are hired

_______
     1/ The City of Ventura work force is large enough to
constitute a significant segment of the public generally.
Since the effect of an across-the-board pay increase or
decrease would be no different for you than any other employee,
then the "public generally" exception found in Section 87103
would apply and no conflict of interest would be found.

Rosanne Coit, Esq.
August 4, 1981
Page Five


and the City Councilmember remains on the council, he or she
may seek our formal written advice prior to any decisions
which may raise a conflict.

I also enclose a copy of a recent Attorney General's
Opinion (No. 80-1204, dated April 9, 1981) for your review.
It deals with a separate subject which may be of interest to
you and your spouse should you be hired.

Sincerely,

Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh
Enclosure

councilmember in creating the new city attorney position. 
Neither you nor the councilmember knew at the time of this 
decision that you would apply. 
Question 2: 
There is an intervening decision-maker in 
the person of the City Attorney, who makes the hiring decision.

so long as the councilmember is, in fact, uninvolved.

cation, etc. -- while the councilmember in question is still

if the councilmember participated. 
This would not be true in the following instances: 
(1) 
If more than 12 months have expired since your 
spouse ceased renting from the councilmember, or otherwise 
being a source of income in excess of $250. 
(2) 
If the council were dealing with city-wide across-
the-board pay increases or decreases, as opposed to your 
position or the City Attorney's Office alone. 
I9/the case 
of such city-wide action, the "public generally"-
exception 
would apply. 
(3) 
If the effect on your income would be so small as 
to not have a material financial effect on your spouse. 
Question 4:

your spouse is not the source of income, it is your spouse 
(or under an alternative analysis you and your spouse). 
We trust that this letter satisfactorily answers your 
questions. 
Should you need clarification please feel free 
to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
In the event you are hired
